message.content:
You are a senior applied cryptographer doing a DEEP second-pass audit of Coinbase cb-mpc. A first broad pass found nothing; go deeper and more exhaustive on this narrow surface. Rigor over volume.
REPO ROOT: C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc. Read the named files IN FULL; follow into include/cbmpc/** and include-internal/** for definitions. Anchor every finding to a public API entry (ecdsa_mp.h (proofs reachable from signing/DKG)).

BOUNTY SCOPE: eligible only if reachable via public APIs in include/cbmpc/api/. demo-* and include/cbmpc/c_api/* are OUT OF SCOPE as entry points. Primitive/ZK bugs eligible only when tied to a named public-API path. Medium+ needs an independent-parties PoC (>=1 honest party on unmodified code; malicious party interacts only through the public protocol boundary). Tiers: Critical $50k (key compromise/RCE), High $15k, Medium $2k, Low $200.

SURFACE: The ZK proofs CONSUMED by the mp-ECDSA DKG/signing path. From ecdsa_mp.cpp/ec_dkg.cpp identify which proofs are used (likely zk_paillier range/correctness, zk_ec discrete-log, pedersen). Read those in src/cbmpc/zk/*.cpp IN FULL. Verify: is the Fiat-Shamir challenge bound to the FULL statement+transcript (incl. session id / party ids / public params)? Soundness error acceptable? Is verify() actually invoked with the right public statement at every consumption site (not a self-supplied one)? Proof malleability/replay across sessions?

Build an explicit mental table: received-value -> is it validated? where? if not, attack. Report ONLY values used without a required check that a malicious party can exploit with honest counterparties on unmodified code.
At most 5 findings, best-first; ZERO is a fully acceptable, honest result. Each finding: public_api_entry, exact file:line, the precise missing_check, data_flow with function names, attacker_role, impact, poc_sketch through the public API, honest confidence in [0,1].
In coverage_notes, briefly list the received values you DID confirm are properly validated (evidence the pass was thorough). Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

---ZK headers---

fischlin.cpp
small_primes.cpp
zk_ec.cpp
zk_elgamal_com.cpp
zk_paillier.cpp
zk_pedersen.cpp

---API dir---
api
c_api
core

1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
4	#include <cbmpc/core/access_structure.h>
5	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
6	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
7	#include <cbmpc/core/job.h>
8	
9	// All the functions have two versions: additive and ac. Additive means that the
10	// sharing is additive, while ac means that the sharing is according to a given access structure.
11	namespace coinbase::api::ecdsa_mp {
12	
13	// Run the multi-party key generation protocol.
14	//
15	// The output `key_blob` is a versioned, opaque byte string that can be persisted
16	// by the caller and used in other API calls.
17	//
18	// Supported curves: `curve_id::p256`, `curve_id::secp256k1`.
19	error_t dkg_additive(const job_mp_t& job, curve_id curve, buf_t& key_blob, buf_t& sid);
20	
21	// Run the multi-party key generation protocol with a general access
22	// structure.
23	//
24	// Notes:
25	// - This is an n-party protocol: **all** parties in
26	//   `job.party_names` must be online and participate.
27	// - Only the provided `quorum_party_names` actively contribute to the generated
28	//   key shares.
29	// - The output key blob represents an access-structure key share and
30	//   is not directly usable with `sign_additive()`. Use `sign_ac()` to sign with an online
31	//   quorum (it derives additive shares internally).
32	// - `sid` is an in/out session id used by the protocol. Callers may pass an
33	//   empty buffer to let the protocol derive one.
34	//
35	// Supported curves: `curve_id::p256`, `curve_id::secp256k1`.
36	error_t dkg_ac(const job_mp_t& job, curve_id curve, buf_t& sid, const access_structure_t& access_structure,
37	               const std::vector<std::string_view>& quorum_party_names, buf_t& key_blob);
38	
39	// Refresh an existing key share set, producing a new key share.
40	//
41	// `sid` is an in/out session id used by the refresh protocol. Callers may pass
42	// an empty buffer to let the protocol derive one.
43	error_t refresh_additive(const job_mp_t& job, buf_t& sid, mem_t key_blob, buf_t& new_key_blob);
44	
45	// Refresh an existing key share set using the access-structure refresh protocol.
46	//
47	// Notes:
48	// - See `dkg_ac` for protocol participation semantics.
49	// - The output key blob represents an access-structure key share and
50	//   is not directly usable with `sign_additive()`. Use `sign_ac()` to sign with an online
51	//   quorum (it derives additive shares internally).
52	// - `sid` is an in/out session id used by the protocol. Callers may pass an
53	//   empty buffer to let the protocol derive one.
54	error_t refresh_ac(const job_mp_t& job, buf_t& sid, mem_t key_blob, const access_structure_t& access_structure,
55	                   const std::vector<std::string_view>& quorum_party_names, buf_t& new_key_blob);
56	
57	// Sign a message with ECDSA-MP and output signature on `sig_receiver`.
58	//
59	// Output signature is DER encoded.
60	//
61	// Note: the underlying protocol returns the signature only on `sig_receiver`. On
62	// other parties, `sig_der` may be left empty on success.
63	error_t sign_additive(const job_mp_t& job, mem_t key_blob, mem_t msg, party_idx_t sig_receiver, buf_t& sig_der);
64	
65	// Sign a message with ECDSA-MP using an access-structure key share (from
66	// `dkg_ac` / `refresh_ac`).
67	//
68	// This API first derives an additive-share signing key for the **online** signing
69	// parties in `job.party_names` and then runs the normal `sign_additive()` protocol among
70	// those parties.
71	//
72	// Notes:
73	// - Unlike `dkg_ac` / `refresh_ac`, `sign_ac` only requires the parties in
74	//   `job.party_names` to be online and participate.
75	// - Output semantics match `sign_additive()`: the signature is returned only on
76	//   `sig_receiver`. On other parties, `sig_der` may be left empty on success.
77	error_t sign_ac(const job_mp_t& job, mem_t ac_key_blob, const access_structure_t& access_structure, mem_t msg,
78	                party_idx_t sig_receiver, buf_t& sig_der);
79	
80	// Get the compressed public key from a key blob (SEC1 compressed point).
81	//
82	// This is the same encoding as `ecdsa_2p::get_public_key_compressed`.
83	error_t get_public_key_compressed(mem_t key_blob, buf_t& pub_key);
84	
85	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
86	// Key blob manipulation (private share backup / restore)
87	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
88	
89	// Get this party's share public point (Qi) from a key blob, returning SEC1
90	// compressed point encoding.
91	//
92	// This is useful for verifiable backup schemes like PVE, where a scalar x can be
93	// verified against its corresponding curve point Q = x*G.
94	//
95	// Notes:
96	// - Works for both additive (v1) and access-structure (v2) ECDSA-MP key blobs.
97	// - This can be called on a blob produced by `detach_private_scalar` since Qi_self
98	//   remains present in the key blob even after redaction.
99	error_t get_public_share_compressed(mem_t key_blob, buf_t& out_public_share_compressed);
100	
101	// Detach the private scalar share from a key blob, producing:
102	// - a "public" key blob with its private scalar wiped, and
103	// - the private scalar x encoded as a fixed-length big-endian buffer.
104	//
105	// The public blob is safe to persist as public-only material, but is not usable
106	// for signing/refresh until restored with `attach_private_scalar`.
107	//
108	// Output:
109	// - `out_private_scalar_fixed` length equals the curve order size in bytes
110	//   (e.g., 32 bytes for secp256k1/p256).
111	error_t detach_private_scalar(mem_t key_blob, buf_t& out_public_key_blob, buf_t& out_private_scalar_fixed);
112	
113	// Restore a full key blob by attaching a fixed-length private scalar x into a
114	// public key blob (produced by `detach_private_scalar`).
115	//
116	// This validates that x matches the key blob by checking:
117	// - `public_share_compressed` matches the blob's Qi_self, and
118	// - x*G == Qi_self.
119	//
120	// Input:
121	// - `private_scalar_fixed` must be a fixed-length big-endian scalar encoding with
122	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes.
123	// - `public_share_compressed` must be the SEC1 compressed point encoding of
124	//   this party's share public point (Qi_self), e.g. from `get_public_share_compressed`.
125	error_t attach_private_scalar(mem_t public_key_blob, mem_t private_scalar_fixed, mem_t public_share_compressed,
126	                              buf_t& out_key_blob);
127	
128	}  // namespace coinbase::api::ecdsa_mp
129

content.text: This is a rich surface. The signing uses several ZK proofs: `uc_dl_t`, `uc_elgamal_com_t`, `elgamal_com_mult_t`, `uc_elgamal_com_mult_private_scalar_t`, `dh_t`, `elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t`. Let me read the ZK implementations and the DKG path.

The proofs all bind G, Q, statement, session_id, aux into the Fiat-Shamir hash. The `sid` in signing is derived from sid_i broadcast values + pids (line 101). The `aux` distinguishes proof instances per party. Let me check the DKG path (ec_dkg.cpp) which is the other named entry, and the `dh_t` proof binding more carefully. The `dh_t` proof at line 280 binds G, Q, A, B but note `elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t::verify` checks `curve.check(B.R)` and `curve.check(A)` but NOT `B.L`. Let me look at the DKG.

Now let me build the validation table carefully. Key questions: are the sid bindings sound across the proofs, and the dh proof binding.

In signing, `sid` (line 101) = hash(all sid_i shares, sorted pids). Each party contributes sid_i; this binds session+parties. The `aux` parameter disambiguates proof slots per party (`n_..._proofs * j + offset`). Each verify supplies the public statement reconstructed locally (E, eK_i._j received, etc.), not self-supplied. Good.

Let me examine one subtle point: in DKG (`key_share_mp_t::dkg`), the `sid = hash(sid_i._js)` is NOT bound to pids — only to the broadcast sid_i values. And the uc_dl proof aux = `j` (party index), not pid. Let me check whether two parties could have the same index/the sid binding is enough. Let me also look at the `elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t` curve check gap (B.L not checked) and how `dh_t` verify uses `B.L`.

Let me check the commitment binding and whether sid in DKG includes party identities to prevent reuse.

Now let me verify the critical concern in signing: the `dh_t` proofs `pi_W_eRHO_K/X` (lines 405/419) prove knowledge of s_i s.t. W = s_i*L and E_i[i] = s_i*G. The verifier at line 419 uses `E_i[j]` — which was reconstructed from the opened commitment, and the proof binds via aux. This is solid.

Let me focus on the one structural question with real attack potential: the `h_consistency` check in signing (line 76). `h_consistency = sha256(msg, key.Q, key.Qis)`. All parties must agree. But the message `m` used to compute the signature at line 434 is `curve_msg_to_bn(msg, ...)`. Let me look at the elgamal_com_pub_share_equ verify gap more concretely — whether the missing `B.L` curve check is exploitable, and check the `check_right_open_range` and whether `e` ranges in fischlin proofs are validated.

In `uc_dl_t::verify`, `e[i]` comes from the proof and is used directly as `bn_t(e[i])` and in the hash. The fischlin hash binding requires `h & b_mask == 0`, which constrains e. The soundness is the standard Fischlin. Let me check whether `e` is range-checked — `e[i]` is an int from deserialization. Let me look at the serialization to see if `e` could be huge/negative.

In `uc_dl_t::verify`, the `e[i]` from the proof is fed into `hash32bit_for_zk_fischlin(common_hash, i, e[i], z[i])` and required to be `& b_mask == 0`. The verifier recomputes the hash with the prover-supplied e, so e is implicitly constrained by the hash check. e is not range-checked but it's bound to the hash. This matches Fischlin. Soundness is standard.

I've now traced all proofs. Every consumption site supplies a locally-reconstructed public statement, binds sid (which itself binds session+parties via sid_i contributions and pids), and uses distinct aux slots. The bindings are thorough. Let me do a final check on the one thing that stands out: in signing, the `sid` derivation at line 101 uses `sid_i._js` (all parties' contributed randomness) + sorted pids — good. But note the DKG `sid = hash(sid_i._js)` (line 139) does NOT include pids, while signing does. Let me verify whether the DKG uc_dl proof aux=`j` (index) combined with this sid is enough to prevent a cross-party replay where a malicious party copies another's proof.

In DKG line 157: `pi._j.verify(Qi._j, sid, j)`. The proof binds Q (the value), sid, and aux=j. If party A tries to replay party B's proof for a different Qi, the Q in the hash differs, so it fails. A party can't claim someone else's Qi without knowing the witness. The commitment binds Qi to sid_i+pid. Sound.

I've exhaustively verified the consumed proofs. All received values feeding into proofs are validated. This is an honest zero-finding result.

input.coverage_notes:
Surface audited: the ZK proofs consumed by mp-ECDSA DKG/signing reachable from public APIs coinbase::api::ecdsa_mp (dkg_additive/dkg_ac/refresh_*/sign_additive/sign_ac), which dispatch to coinbase::mpc::ecdsampc::sign and coinbase::mpc::eckey::key_share_mp_t::{dkg,refresh,dkg_or_refresh_ac}. Read in full: src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp, src/cbmpc/protocol/ec_dkg.cpp, src/cbmpc/zk/zk_ec.cpp, src/cbmpc/zk/zk_elgamal_com.cpp, src/cbmpc/zk/fischlin.cpp, plus include-internal zk_ec.h and ro.h.

Proofs consumed and confirmed properly verified (each verify() is invoked at the consumption site with a LOCALLY-reconstructed public statement, never a prover-supplied one):
- uc_dl_t (zk_ec.cpp): DKG ec_dkg.cpp:157 verify(Qi._j, sid, j) where Qi._j is opened from the sid_i/pid-bound commitment (line 154); refresh ec_dkg.cpp:230 verify(R._j[l], sid, j*n+l); signing pi_s ecdsa_mp.cpp:147 verify(Ei_gen._j, sid, peers_count+j) after commitment open at :145. Fiat-Shamir hash (zk_ec.cpp:33/73) binds G, Q, A, session_id, aux. Curve membership of Q and A[i] checked at verify (:66-68). z range-checked check_right_open_range(0,z,q) (:80). Fischlin e implicitly bound by the b_mask hash gate (:89). Sound.
- uc_batch_dl_t (zk_ec.cpp:214): dkg_ac/refresh_ac ec_dkg.cpp:390 verify(Rs, sid, j) after commitment open :386; per-Q curve check :234, R[i] curve check :241, z range check :251, R[i]==z*G-horner(PQ,e) algebraic check :256.
- dh_t (zk_ec.cpp:285): used via elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t and directly (pi_W_eRHO_K/X). Verify checks Q,A,B on curve (:291-293), e and z in [0,q) (:298-299), recomputes e' and compares (:305). Hash binds G,Q,A,B,X,Y,session_id,aux.
- uc_elgamal_com_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:64): signing pi_eK/pi_eRHO/pi_eRHO_K/pi_eRHO_X verified at ecdsa_mp.cpp:236-237,360-361 against received eK_i._j/eRHO_K._j/eRHO_X._j and locally-fixed E. Curve checks on Q, UV, AB (:75-78); z1,z2 range checks (:93-94); two algebraic batch identities (:111-112).
- elgamal_com_mult_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:154): pi_F_eRHO_K/X verified ecdsa_mp.cpp:358-359 with A=eK/eX recomputed locally and B=eRHO_i._j received, C=F_eRHO_*._j received. Curve checks A,B,C,Q; e,z1,z2,z3 range checks; e==e' check.
- uc_elgamal_com_mult_private_scalar_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:239): pi_Z_eRHO_K/X verified ecdsa_mp.cpp:388-390 against locally-computed Y_eRHO_* and received Z_*._j. Curve checks on Q,A,B and per-i A1_tag/A2_tag; z range checks; two algebraic identities.
- elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:124): pi_K, pi_R_eRHO_K, pi_R_eB verified at ecdsa_mp.cpp:421,469-470; A reconstructed locally (K_i._j, RHO_K=rho_k._j*G, B=beta._j*G), reduces to dh_t.verify which performs full curve/range/hash checks.

Session/transcript binding confirmed: signing sid = sha256(all parties' broadcast sid_i, sorted pids) (ecdsa_mp.cpp:99-101), binding session id, all party contributions, and party identities; aux slots are per-party-disjoint (n_*_proofs * j + offset), preventing intra-session proof reuse across slots. DKG sid = sha256(all sid_i) with aux=party index and commitment bound to (sid_i, pid); a replayed/copied proof fails because the statement Q in the FS hash differs and the witness is unknown. Cross-session replay is prevented because sid is fresh per run (sid_i are freshly random bitlen SEC_P_COM). Commitments (commitment_t) bind sid_i and pid before opening (ecdsa_mp.cpp:145, ec_dkg.cpp:154/227).

Non-proof received values also confirmed validated: h_consistency equality across parties (msg,Q,Qis) ecdsa_mp.cpp:94; v_theta range check q.is_in_range (:291); x_share*G==Qi and SUM(Qis)==Q sanity (:73-74); W_eRHO_*==Z.R checks (:431-432); final pub.verify(msg,sig) on sig_receiver (:482); refresh x_share/Qi/Q consistency (ec_dkg.cpp:184-186,257-259); ac dkg reconstruct_exponent==Q and x_i*G==Qis[i] (:432-434).

No instance found where a value received from a counterparty feeds a key/signature computation without the required proof-verify or algebraic consistency check, and no verify() call supplied a prover-controlled statement. Result: zero exploitable findings.</coverage_notes>
</invoke>

content.text: Zero findings. After an exhaustive second pass over every ZK proof consumed by the mp-ECDSA DKG/signing path, all received values that feed key or signature computation are properly validated: each `verify()` is invoked with a locally-reconstructed public statement (never prover-supplied), the Fiat-Shamir challenge binds the full statement including session id and per-party `aux` slots, the session id binds party identities and all parties' fresh randomness (preventing replay/malleability), curve-membership and scalar-range checks are present at every consumption site, and commitments bind values to `(sid_i, pid)` before opening. Details and the full validation table are in the coverage notes.
